segments.paradiso-4-g69: È is the present indicative of essere, the main verb linking the previous nominal clause to the predicate argomento. Argomento here means "argument" or "evidence"; di fede is a genitive phrase "of (or for) faith." The negative clause non d’eretica nequizia uses elision (d’ = di) and places the adjective eretica before nequizia for rhetorical emphasis: it denies that the matter is due to heretical wickedness.
segments.paradiso-4-g70: Ma perché = "but because." Puote is an archaic or dialectal form of potere (modern può) meaning "can" or "is able to"; here it governs the infinitive penetrare. Vostro accorgimento = "your understanding/insight," where accorgimento is concrete "perception/insight" rather than an "expedient." Ben modifies penetrare to mean "thoroughly" or "well." The preposition a introduces the object of penetration: a questa veritate = "into this truth" (veritate is an archaic spelling of verità).
segments.paradiso-4-g71: Come here means "as" or "in the way that." Disiri is an archaic form of desideri/disiri = "you desire" (second-person singular), so the short parenthetical come disiri means "as you desire/ask."
segments.paradiso-4-g72: Ti is the indirect object "to you." Farò is future indicative of fare ("I will make/do"); here it means "I will make [you]" (i.e. render). Contento is an adjective meaning "content/pleased," so ti farò contento = "I will make you content/pleased."
segments.paradiso-4-g73: Se introduces a conditional ('if'); vïolenza è is the copula clause 'violence is'; quando begins a nominal clause ('when/that which') that defines what Dante means by 'violence'. Quel che is a relative construction 'that which/he who', and pate is an archaic present of 'patire' meaning 'suffers'. Nïente is the negative 'nothing' and conferisce (present indicative of conferire) takes an indirect object introduced by a, here 'a quel che sforza' = 'to him who forces' (sforza = 3rd sing. present of sforzare, 'forces').
segments.paradiso-4-g74: Non fuor is an archaic past-form construction (modern non furono) meaning 'were not'; quest' alme = 'these souls' (quest' is the elided demonstrative before a vowel); per essa means 'by/through that (thing)' (i.e. by that violence) and scusate is a past participle agreeing with alme 'excused/forgiven'.
segments.paradiso-4-g75: Ché here introduces the reason/explanation ('for'/'because'); volontà is the noun 'will' (the human faculty of willing) and is the topic for the following subordinate clause.
segments.paradiso-4-g76: Se non vuol is a short conditional clause: se = 'if' (introducing the condition), non negates, and vuol is the 3rd person singular present of volere ('wants/wills'). The subject (the will) is understood from the preceding segment.
segments.paradiso-4-g77: Non s’ammorza = 'does not extinguish itself' (non + reflexive clitic s' + verb ammorza). Here the reflexive indicates an intransitive passive-like meaning ('is extinguished' / 'dies out'); verb is present indicative and agrees with the feminine subject 'volontà'.
segments.paradiso-4-g78: Ma introduces a contrast ('but'); fa is 3rd person singular present of fare ('does/acts'); come introduces a simile ('as/like'); natura is the subject of the following verb, and face is an archaic present form of 'fare' (from facere) meaning 'acts/does' — so literally 'but (the will) acts as nature acts in fire.' In foco = 'in fire' (poetic order).
segments.paradiso-4-g79: Se mille volte vïolenza il torza = 'even if violence twists it a thousand times.' Se introduces another hypothetical 'if/even if'; mille volte is an adverbial phrase 'a thousand times'; vïolenza is the agent 'violence' and il is an archaic unstressed object pronoun ('it', referring back to the will); torza is a present-form of torcere 'twist' used here in the sense 'twists' (poetic present).
segments.paradiso-4-g80: Per che is an archaic connective meaning 'therefore' or 'for which reason' (a discourse marker introducing the result), here equivalent to 'therefore/so'.
segments.paradiso-4-g81: S’ella si piega assai o poco = 'if it/she yields much or little.' The sequence combines the conditional marker se (here contracted as s'), the subject ella ('it/she' — the will), the reflexive/emphatic si plus the verb piega (3rd sg. present of piegare 'yields, bends'), and the adverbial phrase assai o poco ('much or little').
segments.paradiso-4-g82: Segue la forza = 'force follows' (segue = 3rd pers. sing. present of seguire 'follows'; la forza = 'the force/compulsion'). It states the consequence: where the will yields, force succeeds.
segments.paradiso-4-g83: E così queste ... possendo rifuggir nel santo loco = 'and thus these, being able, flee back into the holy place.' E così = 'and so/thus'; queste is 'these (ones)'; possendo is the gerund of potere ('being able'); rifuggir is an infinitive 'to flee/retreat'; nel santo loco = 'into the holy place.' The gerund + infinitive expresses that, being able to do so, they flee/retreat to the sacred place.
segments.paradiso-4-g27: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a full stop) set off as its own verse/line. It functions as a pause or break in the poem and carries no lexical or grammatical material to parse.
segments.paradiso-4-g28: 'Ancor' is an archaic form of 'ancora' meaning 'still' or 'yet'. The construction 'di dubitar ti dà cagione' literally 'gives you cause to doubt' uses 'ti' as the indirect object ('to you') and 'dà cagione' ('gives cause / provides reason') in the present indicative. The infinitival clause 'parer tornarsi l’anime a le stelle' is a predicative complement: 'parer' (to seem) governs the infinitive 'tornarsi' ('to turn/return themselves') with 'l’anime' (the souls) as its subject and 'a le stelle' as the directional phrase 'to the stars.'
segments.paradiso-4-g29: 'Secondo' here means 'according to' and introduces an authority for the preceding claim. 'La sentenza' is 'the judgment/opinion' and 'di Platone' names Plato as the source. The clause is a straightforward prepositional phrase functioning as a citation of authority.
segments.paradiso-4-g30: 'Queste son' is a simple present copula 'these are.' 'Le question' (plural) is the noun 'the questions.' The relative clause 'che nel tuo velle pontano igualmente' modifies 'le question': 'nel tuo velle' = 'in your will/desire' (tuo + velle, an archaic form for 'will' or 'desire'), 'pontano' (3rd plural present) means 'pose/put forward' or 'arise/are posed,' and 'igualmente' means 'equally' — so the whole clause says these questions are posed equally within your will.
segments.paradiso-4-g31: 'E però' = 'and therefore'; 'pria' (archaic) = 'first' or 'before anything else.' 'Tratterò' is the 1st person singular future 'I will treat/discuss.' 'Quella che più ha di felle' is a relative clause: 'that one which has more of felle'—here 'felle' metaphorically means 'gall' or 'bitterness.' Grammatically, 'quella' refers back to 'question' and 'ha di felle' expresses 'has more bitterness.'
segments.paradiso-4-g32: 'D’i' is the contraction of 'di' + 'i' = 'of the' or 'among the.' 'Colui che' = 'he who' (demonstrative + relative). 'Più s’india' uses the reflexive clitic 's’' and the verb 'indiarsi' (an archaic/poetic verb meaning to become angry/indignant); with 'più' it means 'the one who is most indignant' (comparative/superlative sense). Word order places the descriptive relative clause immediately after 'colui.'
segments.paradiso-4-g33: A single proper name in apposition: 'Moïse' (Moses). It stands as one item in a list of notable figures and requires no additional grammatical marking here.
segments.paradiso-4-g34: Another proper name in the list: 'Samuel.' It functions like the previous name in apposition to the Seraphim mentioned.
segments.paradiso-4-g35: 'E quel Giovanni' = 'and that John' (demonstrative + proper name). The relative clause 'che prender vuoli' uses the infinitive 'prender' ('to take') followed by 'vuoli' (an archaic second-person present 'you wish'); together they mean 'whom you wish to take/choose/accept.' The clause modifies 'quel Giovanni.'
segments.paradiso-4-g36: 'Io dico' is an emphatic parenthetical 'I say' or 'I mean,' used to clarify the speaker's intent about the previous name. 'Io' is the stressed pronoun and 'dico' is present indicative 'I say.'
segments.paradiso-4-g37: 'Non Maria' is a brief corrective parenthesis: 'not Mary.' The negator 'non' directly precedes the proper name to exclude her from the referent just named.
segments.paradiso-4-g38: The structure 'non hanno ... che' expresses exception: 'they do not have ... other than.' 'Non hanno in altro cielo i loro scanni' = 'they do not have their seats in another heaven' and 'che questi spirti che mo t’appariro' is the restrictive clause 'except these spirits which now appear to you.' 'Appariro' is a poetic/pastive form meaning 'appear' (here present sense).
segments.paradiso-4-g39: 'Né' begins a negative continuation 'nor.' 'Hanno a l'esser lor più o meno anni' literally 'they have, in their being, more or fewer years' — an idiom meaning there is no difference of age among them; 'a l'esser lor' = 'to/for their being' and 'più o meno anni' = 'more or fewer years.' The clause uses 'hanno' in a possessive sense about state/age.
segments.paradiso-4-g40: 'Ma tutti fanno bello il primo giro' — 'ma' = 'but'; 'tutti' = 'all (of them)'; 'fanno' (3rd pl. of 'fare') = 'make' or 'render'; 'bello' = 'beautiful' or 'fair'; 'il primo giro' = 'the first circle/orbit.' Grammatically, the verb 'fanno' takes 'il primo giro' as its direct object and 'bello' as object complement.
segments.paradiso-4-g41: 'E differentemente han dolce vita' = 'and differently they have/enjoy sweet life' — 'differentemente' (differently) modifies the clause. 'Per sentir più e men l’etterno spiro' gives the cause/manner: 'by/through feeling the eternal breath more or less.' Here 'sentir' is used in the sense 'to feel' or 'to experience' and 'spiro' is 'breath' (eternal breath), with 'più e men' expressing degrees (more or less).
segments.paradiso-4-g100: A temporal adverbial phrase: 'Molte fïate' (with the diaeresis marking the vowel break in fïate) means 'many times', and già adds 'already' or 'formerly' to situate the action in past experience. Word order is the typical adverbial fronting that sets the scene.
segments.paradiso-4-g101: Vocative address: 'frate' is a direct address meaning 'brother' (not necessarily a religious title here) and is set off by the following comma.
segments.paradiso-4-g102: 'Addivenne' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'addivenire' used impersonally: 'it happened'. The conjunction 'che' introduces the subordinate clause that reports what happened.
segments.paradiso-4-g103: 'Per fuggir periglio' uses per + infinitive ('fuggir' = fuggire) to state purpose: 'in order to flee/escape danger.' 'Periglio' is the direct object of the verb notion 'to flee'.
segments.paradiso-4-g104: Complex clause: 'contra grato' means 'against (his) liking' with the adjective following the preposition. 'Si fé' is reflexive/passive-like (si + past 'fé' = 'he made himself' / 'he became'); 'di quel che far non si convenne' is a relative clause: 'of that which doing was not fitting' — 'convenne' is passato remoto of 'convenire' used impersonally ('it was fitting'). The negative 'non' negates the infinitive 'far'.
segments.paradiso-4-g105: 'Come' here functions as a simile particle meaning 'like' or 'as'. 'Almeone' in the text acts as the example introduced by the simile (a proper name in context).
segments.paradiso-4-g106: The relative pronoun 'che' introduces the relative clause modifying 'Almeone' and here means 'who'.
segments.paradiso-4-g107: 'Di ciò' ('about/for this') introduces the matter; 'pregato' is a past participle of 'pregare' used adjectivally/passively meaning 'having been begged/urged'. The agent is marked by 'dal padre suo' = 'by his father'.
segments.paradiso-4-g108: 'La propria madre' is the direct object ('his own mother' — 'propria' emphasizes possession). 'Spense' is an archaic past-tense verb (passato remoto) used transitively here: in context it means 'put to death' or 'put out' (i.e. killed).
segments.paradiso-4-g109: 'Per non perder pietà' gives purpose/result: 'so as not to lose pity/compassion' (per + non + infinitive). 'Si fé spietato' again uses reflexive si + past 'fé' + adjective 'spietato' to mean 'he made himself / became pitiless' — a common idiom for willful change of disposition.
segments.paradiso-4-g110: A questo punto is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at this point' (A = a + prepositional sense 'at'). Voglio introduces a desiderative clause and is followed by che + subjunctive; che tu pense uses the present subjunctive (tu pense) after voglio che to express 'I want that you think/consider'. La forza al voler si mischia is a separate subordinate clause started by the second che: la forza is the subject, al = a + il is the preposition + article 'to the', voler is a verbal noun 'will', and si mischia is present indicative with the reflexive si ('mixes itself' / 'is mixed with').
segments.paradiso-4-g111: This continues the previous clause: e fanno (they make/do; plural because la forza and il voler are treated together) introduces a result clause. Sì che begins the result clause 'so that', scusar is an infinitive used as the verb in an impersonal/passive-reflexive construction, non ... si posson expresses inability with the archaic posson (modern possono) and si as the impersonal/reflexive marker, and l’offense is the direct object 'the offence(s)'.
segments.paradiso-4-g112: Voglia assoluta is a nominal phrase 'absolute will/ desire' (voglia as a noun). Non consente is present indicative with non to negate; consente (from consentire) means 'consent' or 'allow'. Al danno is 'to/for harm' (al = a + il). The clause is straightforward subject–verb–prepositional object.
segments.paradiso-4-g113: Ma introduces contrast 'but'. Consentevi is an older/dialectal form where the clitic vi (= 'to it' or 'in it') is attached to consente (it consents/permits it); the verb is present indicative. In tanto in quanto is a correlative expression 'insofar as' or 'only insofar as', and teme is present indicative 'it fears' (the absolute will 'fears').
segments.paradiso-4-g114: Se is the conditional conjunction 'if'. Si is the reflexive/impersonal clitic referring back to the will (itself), and ritrae is present indicative of ritrarre 'withdraw, pull back' (archaic spelling ritrae). The clause is protasis ('if it withdraws').
segments.paradiso-4-g115: Cadere is an infinitive 'to fall' used here as the main verb of the apodosis introduced by the prior se-clause; in older Italian infinitives often continue result clauses. In più affanno = 'into greater anguish' (in + più 'more/greater' + affanno 'anguish').
segments.paradiso-4-g116: Però here functions as a discourse connector meaning 'however' or 'therefore' depending on context; it introduces the following clarification or consequence.
segments.paradiso-4-g117: Quando is 'when'. Piccarda is the proper name (subject). Quello is a demonstrative 'that' referring to the point just discussed. Spreme is present indicative of spreme(re) 'presses/exacts'; figuratively 'presses the matter' or 'puts the question'.
segments.paradiso-4-g118: De (archaic for di) + la = 'of the' or 'about the'; la voglia assoluta is the noun phrase 'the absolute will'; intende is present indicative of intendere 'understands' or 'has in mind'. The clause means 'she understands the absolute will'.
segments.paradiso-4-g119: E io de l’altra is coordinate: e io 'and I', de l’altra = di + l' 'of the other' meaning 'I (understand) the other [will]'. The verb (intende) is understood from the previous clause — a common elliptical construction.
segments.paradiso-4-g120: Sì che introduces the result 'so that' or 'thus', ver is an adverb 'truly' or 'really', diciamo is present indicative 'we say', and insieme 'together' — together: 'so that we truly say together.'
segments.paradiso-4-g1: 'Intra' is an archaic/prepositional use here meaning 'between'; it governs the plural noun 'due cibi' (two foods), so the phrase reads 'between two foods'. Word order is straightforward preposition + numeral + noun.
segments.paradiso-4-g2: Two coordinate adjectives 'distanti e moventi' modify 'cibi'. 'Distanti' = 'distant' (physically or in approach) and 'moventi' is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'moving' or 'enticing' ('that which moves' in the given senses). The phrase 'd’un modo' ('of one manner') is a genitive-of-manner meaning 'in the same way'.
segments.paradiso-4-g3: 'Prima' = 'before'; 'si morria' is an impersonal/hypothetical construction ('one would die' or 'someone would die') with 'morria' functioning as a conditional/imperfect-form verb expressing the hypothetical consequence. 'Di fame' = 'of hunger'.
segments.paradiso-4-g4: 'Che' introduces the subordinate clause ('that...'); 'liber omo' is a poetic inversion for 'libero omo' = 'any free man' (adjective before noun). 'L’un' = 'l'uno' ('one of them'), the object; 'recasse' is a past conditional/subjunctive form of 'recar'/'recar(e)' (to bring) here meaning 'would bring' or 'would take'; 'ai denti' literally 'to the teeth' = 'to his teeth' (i.e. to bite).
segments.paradiso-4-g5: 'Sì si starebbe' is an impersonal/conditional expression: 'so would stand' or 'so would find himself' (literally 'would be standing'); 'un agno' is the subject ('a lamb'); 'intra due brame' = 'between two desires' and 'di fieri lupi' is a genitive/possessive phrase 'of fierce wolves' modifying 'brame'. The whole clause is a simile continuing the prior hypothetical.
segments.paradiso-4-g6: 'Igualmente' = 'equally'; 'temendo' is a present participle ('fearing'), indicating the lamb's simultaneous attitude — 'fearing both equally.' The participle qualifies the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-4-g7: A parallel simile: 'sì si starebbe un cane intra due dame' = 'so a dog would stand between two ladies.' Grammar mirrors the earlier 'sì si starebbe' construction; 'intra due dame' is 'between two ladies.'
segments.paradiso-4-g8: 'Per che' here functions as a discourse connector meaning 'therefore' or 'for which reason,' introducing the speaker's conclusion based on the preceding similes.
segments.paradiso-4-g9: This is conditional/hypothetical: 's’i’ mi tacea' = 'if I had been silent' (Dante contracts 'se io' to 's'i' before the verb). 'Mi tacea' (imperfect) here expresses 'I kept quiet' or 'I had been silent.' Note the clitic order and contraction typical of older Italian/poetic style.
segments.paradiso-4-g10: 'Me non riprendo' = 'I do not reproach/blame myself.' 'Riprendo' is first-person present (or present historic) of 'riprendere' used reflexively here with 'me' to mean 'reproach myself' or 'take myself to task.' The negation 'non' negates the verb.
segments.paradiso-4-g11: 'Da li miei dubbi' = 'from my doubts' (agent/source); 'd’un modo sospinto' = 'pushed/driven in one way' where 'd'un modo' ('in one manner') qualifies 'sospinto' (past participle 'pushed'). Together this participial phrase gives the reason/cause: 'having been driven by my doubts in one way.'
segments.paradiso-4-g12: 'Poi ch’era necessario' = 'since/after it was necessary.' 'Poi che' is a causal connector here; 'era' is the imperfect indicative 'was' and 'necessario' = 'necessary,' stating the necessity that justifies the silence or action.
segments.paradiso-4-g13: 'Né' = 'nor' or 'neither'; 'commendo' is first-person singular present of a verb meaning 'I commend' or 'I praise' (Dante uses it to mean he does not commend/praise the action). The clause denies that he praises it.
segments.paradiso-4-g14: 'Io mi tacea' = 'I was silent' or 'I kept silent.' 'Tacea' is the imperfect of 'tacere' ('to be silent'), and the reflexive clitic 'mi' emphasizes the subject: 'I myself was silent.'
segments.paradiso-4-g15: 'Ma' = 'but' introduces contrast. ''L mio disir dipinto m'era nel viso' literally 'my desire, painted, was on my face.' 'Dipinto' is a past participle used adjectivally ('depicted/pictured'); 'm'era' ('era' with clitic 'mi') indicates 'it was upon me / on my face' — idiomatic placement of feeling on the visage.
segments.paradiso-4-g16: 'E 'l dimandar con ello' = 'and the asking with it' (i.e. the request/plea that went along with that desire). 'Dimandar' here is a noun/verb-noun 'the asking/request'; 'con ello' = 'with it' (ello refers back to the desire/its expression on the face).
segments.paradiso-4-g17: 'Più caldo assai che per parlar distinto' is a comparative: 'much warmer (more ardent) than by distinct/explicit speech.' 'Più ... assai' intensifies the comparative; 'che per' introduces the comparison 'than by/through distinct speaking'; 'distinto' = 'clear, distinct' (speech).
segments.paradiso-4-g18: 'Fé' is the archaic past tense of 'fare' (made/did). 'Fé sì Beatrice qual fé Danïello' = 'Beatrice did as Daniel did' (the pattern 'fé sì X qual fé Y' uses 'qual' = 'as' to compare actions). Word order is inverted as typical in poetry.
segments.paradiso-4-g19: 'Nabuccodonosor levando d'ira' is a participial phrase: 'Nebuchadnezzar, rising/acting from anger' ('levando' present participle meaning 'raising/being moved by'). It sets the circumstance in which Daniel acted.
segments.paradiso-4-g20: 'Che' introduces a relative/explanatory clause: 'who had made him unjustly fello.' 'L'avea fatto' = 'had made him' (pluperfect construction); 'ingiustamente' = 'unjustly'; 'fello' = 'cruel/villain' (an adjective/noun describing the transformed person).
segments.paradiso-4-g21: 'E disse:' = 'and said:' — 'disse' is the past historic (simple past) of 'dire' used to introduce direct speech.
segments.paradiso-4-g22: In the direct speech, 'Io veggio ben' = 'I see well' (here 'veggio' is an archaic/poetic form of 'veggo'/'vedo' — 'I see'); 'come ti tira uno e altro disio' = 'how one desire and another pulls you.' 'Ti tira' literally 'pulls you' (present indicative) describing being drawn by conflicting desires. Note some listed dictionary senses do not reflect this archaic meaning, so the semantic mapping is adjusted.
segments.paradiso-4-g23: 'Sì che' introduces a result clause: 'so that' or 'thus that.' 'Tua cura sé stessa lega' = 'your care/concern binds itself' ('lega' present indicative 'binds'); the reflexive 'sé stessa' emphasizes that the care binds itself. 'Sì che fuor non spira' = 'so that it does not breathe out' (i.e. cannot escape or be freed). 'Spira' here is used in the sense 'give out breath/escape' rather than 'spiral.'
segments.paradiso-4-g24: 'Tu argomenti:' = 'You object' or 'You argue:' — here 'argomenti' is a verbal form ('you argue') used to introduce the position Beatrice is about to address, even though the provided noun senses list is for a noun form.
segments.paradiso-4-g25: 'Se 'l buon voler dura' = 'If the good will/enduring good intention lasts.' 'Se' = 'if' (conditional particle); 'l buon voler' = 'the good will' or 'good intent' (noun phrase); 'dura' is present indicative 'lasts/endures.'
segments.paradiso-4-g26: The question proper: 'la vïolenza altrui per qual ragione di meritar mi scema la misura?' Grammatically the order is inverted: 'la violenza altrui' (another's violence) is the subject; 'per qual ragione' = 'for what reason'; 'di meritar' ('of deserving' / 'to deserve') depends on 'scema' (diminishes); 'mi' indicates 'to me' (my), and 'la misura' = 'the measure' (my due measure). 'Scema' is present indicative 'diminishes' — the speaker asks why someone else's violent act should lessen his deserved measure.
segments.paradiso-4-g84: This is a conditional protasis introduced by Se + a compound subjunctive: fosse stato is the past subjunctive (congiuntivo trapassato) of essere used to express an unreal past condition: 'if ... had been'. lor is the archaic clitic for loro = 'their' placed before the noun volere ('will/desire'), and intero is an adjective postposed to volere (typical word order) meaning 'whole/complete'.
segments.paradiso-4-g85: come introduces an example/simile 'as' and tenne is the past historic (tenne) of tenere = 'held/kept'. Proper name Lorenzo follows the verb as subject; in su la grada is a prepositional phrase with in + su for emphasis 'upon the step' (su strengthened by in). The article la marks grada ('the step/tier').
segments.paradiso-4-g86: e links the two examples. fece is the past historic of fare = 'made/did'; Muzio is the subject. a la sua man is an idiomatic locution where a + la man indicates manner or direction ('with / in his hand'); sua is the possessive 'his' modifying man (hand). severo is an adjective 'stern', describing the manner of Muzio's action.
segments.paradiso-4-g87: così refers back to the previous examples 'thus/so', and l’ is the elided article referring to the object (here 'them'); avria is the conditional auxiliary (condizionale passato periphrastic form: avria + past participle) = 'would have'. ripinte is a past participle (feminine/plural) agreeing with the object 'them' and here means 'driven back/painted again' in a figurative sense. per la strada marks the path along which the action would have occurred.
segments.paradiso-4-g88: come begins a subordinate clause 'as' and fuoro is the archaic past (passato remoto) of essere = 'were'. sciolte is a past participle (feminine plural) from sciogliere 'loosen/release'; here it describes the prior state 'as they had been loosened/released'.
segments.paradiso-4-g89: ma 'but' introduces a contrast. così salda voglia = 'so/so much a steady/firm desire' where salda (from saldo) modifies voglia (desire/will). è is the present of essere 'is', troppo 'too' modifies the adjective rada 'rare/sparse', so the clause means 'but such a steadfast will is too rare'.
segments.paradiso-4-g90: E per queste parole is a simple connective phrase: E = 'and', per = 'because of/on account of', queste parole = 'these words' (demonstrative + noun). It functions as a frame for what follows.
segments.paradiso-4-g91: This clause has inverted word order for metrical reasons: ricolte is a past participle 'gathered/collected' and l’ (clitic) + hai is the compound passato prossimo 'you have [them]'. se at the start is the conditional 'if'. come dei is a simile 'as ...' (dei here is ambiguous in isolation but syntactically follows come).
segments.paradiso-4-g92: è is the present of essere 'is', l’argomento 'the argument' (article + noun). casso is an attributive adjective meaning 'vain/void' (here 'vain/useless'). che introduces a relative clause: t’avria fatto is a conditional perfect periphrasis (t’ = to you, avria = would have, fatto = done/made) and noia = 'annoyance/trouble'; ancor più volte = 'still/more often'. Altogether: 'the argument is vain that would have given you even more annoyance'.
segments.paradiso-4-g93: Ma or = 'but now' (or = now). The sequence ti s’attraversa uses the clitic ti (to you) plus pronominal si in s’attraversa (si attraversa) with the verb attraversare in present indicative: literally 'it crosses/blocks itself to you' → 'it crosses/blocks you'. un altro passo is 'another passage/step' and dinanzi a li occhi = 'before your eyes' (dinanzi + a + article li + occhi). The reflexive/impersonal si makes the clause idiomatic: 'an obstacle presents itself to you'.
segments.paradiso-4-g94: tal che = 'such that' introduces a result clause. per te stesso = 'by yourself' or 'for yourself' (te stesso emphasizes 'you personally'). non usciresti is the conditional present (or an archaic future usage) of uscire: 'you would not get out/escape', so the clause states the consequence of the obstacle.
segments.paradiso-4-g95: pria is an archaism meaning 'before/first'. saresti is the conditional of essere 'you would be' (used here as resultative with pria), and lasso is an adjective 'weary/exhausted' describing the expected state: 'you would first be weary'.
segments.paradiso-4-g96: Io t’ho per certo ne la mente messo = literally 'I have put it in my mind for certain to you' — idiomatically 'I have firmly placed in my mind, and told you, that...'. Io t’ho is the clitic construction (ti ho = 'I have to you'); per certo = 'for certain/firmly'; ne la mente messo is a past participle phrase 'placed in (my) mind'. The following ch' introduces the content clause.
segments.paradiso-4-g97: Però ch’è sempre al primo vero appresso = 'because/for it is always close to the first truth.' Però ch' = causal connector 'because/for', è = 'is', sempre = 'always', al primo vero appresso = 'close/near to the first Truth' (primo vero = ultimate truth/God; appresso = near/close).
segments.paradiso-4-g98: E poi potesti da Piccarda udire = 'and then you were able to hear from Piccarda' (potesti = 2nd pers. sing. past of potere 'you could/you were able'; da Piccarda = 'from Piccarda'; udire = infinitive 'to hear' used with potesti). Che l’affezion del vel Costanza tenne = 'that the affection/attachment of the veil Constancy held' — l’affezion = 'the attachment/affection'; del vel is an archaic phrase (vel likely stands for 'veil' or is an old form) and Costanza here reads either as the noun 'constancy' or a personified name; tenne is past of tenere 'kept/held'. The clause reports what you heard from Piccarda.
segments.paradiso-4-g99: Sì ch’ella par qui meco contradire = 'so that she seems here to contradict me.' Sì ch' = 'so that/therefore that'; ella = 'she' (Piccarda); par = 3rd pers. sing. present of parere 'seems'; qui = 'here/at this point'; meco = 'with me' (older form of 'con me'); contradire = infinitive 'to contradict'.
segments.paradiso-4-g55: Dice is 3rd-person singular present indicative ('he/she/it says'); che introduces the subordinate content clause. L'alma uses the elided article + noun ('the soul') as subject, and a la sua stella is a prepositional phrase ('to its/their own star') placed before the verb riede, which is 3rd-person singular present of riedere 'return' (inverted word order is normal in poetry).
segments.paradiso-4-g56: Credendo is a gerund ('believing'), giving the manner or motive for the assertion. Quella is a demonstrative pronoun ('that one' / 'that'), quindi functions here as 'therefore' or 'thus', and esser decisa is the infinitive construction with essere + past participle (decisa = 'determined/decided')—i.e. 'to be determined'. Quando introduces a temporal clause; per forma is 'by/in virtue of form', la is the object pronoun, and diede is passato remoto (3rd sg) 'gave'.
segments.paradiso-4-g57: E 'and'; forse is an adverb 'perhaps'. Sua sentenza is a possessive + noun ('his/its judgment/opinion'), è d'altra guisa literally 'is of another sort' (d' = di + definite article elided). Che introduces the following clause; la voce non suona is a simple negative clause where suona is 3rd-person present of suonare, here meaning 'sounds / utters / expresses'.
segments.paradiso-4-g58: Ed = and (poetic variant of e). Esser puote is a raised infinitive plus an archaic/present form meaning 'it may be / it can be' (puote ~ può). Con intenzion ('with intention') is an ablative/instrumental phrase describing motive; da + infinitive (da non esser derisa) expresses purpose/aim ('so as not to be derided'), with derisa a past participle used adjectivally ('mocked').
segments.paradiso-4-g59: S'elli is a poetic contraction of 'se elli' ('if he/they'), introducing a conditional clause; intende is 3rd-person present ('he intends' or 'means'), tornare is the infinitive 'to return/turn back' and governs the following prepositional phrase a queste ruote ('to these wheels' — i.e. the spheres/wheels of the heavens). L'onor de la influenza e 'l biasmo is the coordinated direct object ('the honor of influence and the blame').
segments.paradiso-4-g60: Forse = perhaps. In alcun vero suo arco is a circumstantial prepositional phrase: in + alcun ('in some') + vero ('true/real') + suo arco ('his bow/arc') — here arco most naturally 'bow' or 'bow-stroke' in a metaphorical sense. Percuote is 3rd-person present indicative of percuotere 'strikes' (can be literal or figurative); the subject is carried over from the conditional clause.
segments.paradiso-4-g61: Questo is the demonstrative 'this' and principio is the noun 'principle/origin'; they form a simple nominal phrase functioning as the sentence subject. The comma sets off the following participial phrase.
segments.paradiso-4-g62: Male is an adverb 'badly/ill', and inteso is the past participle of intendere used adjectivally ('understood'); together they form a reduced clause meaning 'being badly understood' or 'misunderstood'.
segments.paradiso-4-g63: Torse is the passato remoto (3rd-singular) of torcere (here meaning 'twisted/turned/corrupted'); già means 'already' and tutto il mondo quasi = 'almost the whole world' (quasi qualifying the whole phrase). The verb is a past, narrative tense (passato remoto), typical in literary Italian.
segments.paradiso-4-g64: Sì che is a result clause marker 'so that/so much so that'; Giove is the proper name 'Jupiter' and functions as one item in the following list affected by the result.
segments.paradiso-4-g65: Mercurio e Marte are coordinate proper names. A + infinitive (a nominar) introduces the action 'in naming' or 'to name/mention', and trascorse is passato remoto (3rd-singular) of trascorrere used here in the sense 'passed over' or 'skipped' — i.e. one passed over even naming those planets.
segments.paradiso-4-g66: L'altra = the other (with elided article); dubitazion is the noun 'doubt' (archaic spelling without final -e). Che ti commove is a relative clause ('that moves you' — commove is present indicative), and ha men velen is a simple clause where ha (has) takes velen ('venom/poison/malice') as object, modified by men (archaic 'less' = meno).
segments.paradiso-4-g67: Però che introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'), sua malizia = 'its malice', and the clause non ti poria menar is a conditional/modal construction: poria is an archaic/modal form meaning 'would be able'/'could', with menar (infinitive) 'to lead' and ti the indirect/object pronoun 'you'. Da me altrove is a prepositional phrase 'from me to elsewhere' = 'away from me to another place.'
segments.paradiso-4-g42: 'Qui' is an adverb meaning 'here' placed before the verb for emphasis. 'si' is the reflexive/clitic pronoun and with 'mostraro' makes the verb pronominal: 'mostraro' is passato remoto (3rd person plural) of mostrar(e) — a poetic form meaning 'they showed themselves' or 'they appeared'.
segments.paradiso-4-g43: This negative causal clause uses 'non perché ... sia' — 'non perché' (= 'not because') + present subjunctive 'sia' (3rd sg. of essere) to deny the supposed cause. 'sortita' is a past participle (from sortire) used with 'sia' ('may be come forth' / 'has arisen'); 'questa spera lor' = 'this sphere of theirs' (spera = sphere; 'lor' = their).
segments.paradiso-4-g44: 'Ma' = 'but'. 'Per' here means 'in order to' (purpose), followed by the infinitive 'far' ('to make') and the noun 'segno' = 'a sign'. 'De la celestïal' = 'of the celestial (one)', and 'c’ ha' is a contracted relative ('che ha' = 'which has'); 'men salita' = 'less ascent' (men = less, salita = ascent). The clause describes the thing they indicate: the celestial sphere that has risen less.
segments.paradiso-4-g45: 'Così parlar' uses the infinitive 'parlar' as a verbal noun ('to speak thus' / 'such speech'). 'Conviensi' is an impersonal/archaic 3rd-person form of 'conviene' meaning 'it is fitting' or 'one ought'; it takes the indirect object 'al vostro ingegno' = 'for your intellect'.
segments.paradiso-4-g46: 'Però che' functions here as 'because' introducing the reason. 'Solo da sensato' = 'only from what is sensible' (da + adjective used substantively). 'Apprende' is 3rd-person singular present 'one learns' (general/indefinite subject). The relative clause 'ciò che fa poscia d’intelletto degno' means 'that which afterwards makes [one] worthy of the intellect': 'fa' = 'makes/causes', 'poscia' = 'afterwards', 'd’(intelletto) degno' = 'worthy of intellect.'
segments.paradiso-4-g47: 'Per questo' = 'for this reason'. 'La Scrittura' (Scripture) is the subject and 'condescende' (present tense) means 'stoops' or 'accommodates' — here 'condescendere a [qualcosa]' = 'to assent to / to adapt to' — and it takes 'a vostra facultate' ('to/for your faculty/ability') as indirect object.
segments.paradiso-4-g48: The coordinate clause lists what Scripture does: 'e piedi e mano attribuisce a Dio' = 'and attributes feet and hand to God' ('attribuisce' = third-person present 'attributes/assigns'). 'E altro intende' is elliptical: 'and it intends/meant something else' — 'intende' = 'means/has in mind' (not 'understand' here).
segments.paradiso-4-g49: 'E Santa Chiesa' = 'and the Holy Church' (Santa = 'holy' as adjective). 'Con aspetto umano' = 'with a human appearance' (prepositional phrase modifying how the Church represents). 'Gabrïel e Michel vi rappresenta' uses 'vi' as a dative/clitic meaning 'to you' (it presents them to human understanding/audience), and 'rappresenta' = 'represents/presents' (3rd sg.).
segments.paradiso-4-g50: Short clause: 'e l’altro che Tobia rifece sano.' 'L’altro' = 'the other (angel)' and the relative clause 'che Tobia rifece sano' uses 'rifece' (passato remoto, 3rd sg. of rifare) meaning 'restored/made whole' and 'sano' = 'healthy.' The subject of 'rifece' is the implied 'l’altro' (the angel who healed Tobias).
segments.paradiso-4-g51: 'Quel che Timeo de l’anime argomenta' = 'What Timeo (Timaeus) argues about the souls'; 'de l’anime' = 'of the souls' (de = of). 'Argomenta' is 3rd-person present 'argues, maintains'. The main clause 'non è simile a ciò che qui si vede' uses 'si vede' as an impersonal/passive construction ('one sees' / 'is seen here'), so the sense is 'is not similar to what is seen here.'
segments.paradiso-4-g52: 'Però che' introduces the causal/explanatory clause here ('because'), a common medieval usage where 'però' functions like 'perché'.
segments.paradiso-4-g53: 'Come dice' = 'as he says' or 'as it is said' — 'come' = 'as' and 'dice' = 'says' (3rd sg.). It prepares the following parenthetical remark.
segments.paradiso-4-g54: 'Par che senta' is a classic 'seem' + subjunctive construction: 'par' (short for pare/paret) = 'it seems', followed by the subjunctive 'senta' (3rd sg. of sentire) — 'it seems that he perceives/feels.' The subjunctive after 'par che' signals appearance/uncertainty about the perceived state.
segments.paradiso-4-g139: Nasce is a present indicative verb (literally 'is born' or 'arises') used impersonally here: the subject ('il dubbio' later in the line) is what 'arises' from the stated cause. Per + quello marks the origin or source ('from/through that'), with quello ('that') referring back to the prior idea.
segments.paradiso-4-g140: a guisa di is a fixed comparative formula meaning 'in the manner of' or 'like'; guisa is a noun 'manner' and di introduces the comparison. Rampollo is a metaphorical noun 'young shoot, scion' that completes the simile.
segments.paradiso-4-g141: a piè (literally 'at the foot') is a prepositional phrase: a + piè = 'at the foot of'. Del = di + il links to vero, here a noun meaning 'truth' rather than an adjective. The phrase a piè del vero locates the rampollo (the simile) 'at the foot of truth', while il dubbio is the grammatical subject carried to the end for poetic emphasis.
segments.paradiso-4-g142: Ed è natura is a copular clause: ed (e before a vowel) + è (present of essere) + natura ('nature' or human constitution) stating a general truth. Ch' introduces a relative clause ('that/which'), al sommo is 'to the summit' (a + il contracted), and pinge is the finite verb of that relative clause. Noi is the direct object 'us' and di collo in collo is an idiomatic locution meaning 'from neck to neck' (a step-by-step or stage-by-stage motion).
segments.paradiso-4-g143: Questo is a demonstrative pronoun 'this' (referring to the preceding point). m' is the clitic for mi 'me' (direct or indirect object here) attached to invita, which is present indicative 'urges/entices' or 'invites' — here 'urges' or 'encourages' the speaker to act.
segments.paradiso-4-g144: A second repetition of questo emphasizes the motive; m' (mi) is again the clitic object. Assicura is present indicative 'assures' or 'reassures' and con reverenza is an adverbial phrase ('with reverence/respect') modifying how she assures him.
segments.paradiso-4-g145: Donna is a vocative here: the speaker addresses the lady (Beatrice). In medieval poetry this vocative often carries a respectful or beloved sense.
segments.paradiso-4-g146: a + infinitive dimandarvi = 'to ask you' (a introduces purpose: 'to do something'); dimandarvi is dimandare + the enclitic vi 'to you' meaning 'ask of you'. D'un'altra verità = 'of another truth' (di + un' + altra + verità), and che m'è oscura is a relative clause 'which is obscure to me' with m' = 'to me' and è = 'is' followed by the adjective oscura.
segments.paradiso-4-g147: Io vo' saper is an idiom meaning 'I want to know' (vo' is a contraction of voglio in Tuscan/poetic usage). Saper is the infinitive 'to know' that follows the verb of volition. Se introduces an indirect yes/no question 'whether'. L'uom (l' + uom) is the subject 'man', può is present indicative 'can/is able to', and sodisfarvi is sodisfare + enclitic vi = 'to satisfy you' or 'to make amends to you'. Ai voti manchi: ai (a + i) 'to the/vs the vows', voti 'vows', and manchi functions as an adjective/participle 'lacking' modifying voti. Sì con altri beni = 'so/thus with other goods'.
segments.paradiso-4-g148: Ch' is the conjunction 'that' introducing a subordinate purpose/result clause. A la vostra statera = 'to/for your balance/scale' (a + la + vostra + statera), non is the negation 'not', sien is the present subjunctive of essere (third-person plural subjunctive 'they be'), and parvi (from parere + clitic -vi) means 'seem to you' or 'seem appropriate/fitting to you' — here 'not be fitting/seem to you'. The subjunctive sien is used after ch' expressing a (potential) result or evaluation.
segments.paradiso-4-g149: Beatrice is the proper name and the subject; mi is the clitic object 'me' and guardò is passato remoto (simple past) 'looked at'. Con + li occhi pieni is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with eyes full (of...)', di faville d'amor specifies what fills them ('sparks of love'), and così divini is an adverb + adjective phrase modifying the sparks 'so divine'.
segments.paradiso-4-g150: Che here introduces the result/consequence clause that follows ('so that' / 'so that, ...'). It functions as a relative/conjunction linking the previous visual image to its effect on the poet.
segments.paradiso-4-g151: Vinta is a past participle (feminine agreement with an implied 'her gaze' or with 'Beatrice' as agent) functioning as an absolute/participial phrase meaning 'being overcome' or 'overcome'. It sets up the circumstance under which the main clause that follows occurs.
segments.paradiso-4-g152: Mia virtute is an ablative/agent-like phrase 'my virtue/strength'; diè is passato remoto of dare 'gave' used idiomatically with le reni: diè le reni = 'gave the loins' meaning 'my strength gave way in my loins' (an idiom for losing strength). Le reni = 'the loins/loins area' (plural).
segments.paradiso-4-g153: E quasi ('and almost') introduces the following personal reaction: mi perdei is passato remoto first person singular 'I lost myself' or 'I was almost lost/overcome', and con li occhi chini is an instrumental phrase 'with eyes lowered/bowed' describing his physical posture as he is overcome.
segments.paradiso-4-g121: Cotal fu = literary/poetic inversion meaning "such was" (cotal = 'such', fu = past tense of essere). l’ondeggiar is an infinitive used as a noun ('the undulating/motion'), del santo rio = genitive 'of the holy stream.' ch’uscì = ch’ (che) + uscì, past tense 'that issued/flowed out.' del fonte = 'from the spring' (del = di + il). ond’ is a contracted 'onde' or 'ond' meaning 'whence/from which'; ogne ver deriva = 'every truth derives/originates' (deriva = present indicative 'flows/originates from'). Overall the clause describes the motion of the sacred river that comes out of the source from which all truth comes.
segments.paradiso-4-g122: tal = 'such' (echoing cotal), puose = past tense of porre/porre-variant 'pose/placed' (he/it placed), in pace = 'in peace/calm,' uno e altro disio = 'one and the other desire' (uno ... e altro enumerates two desires). The verb puose governs the prepositional phrase and the coordinated direct objects.
segments.paradiso-4-g123: O is the vocative interjection 'O/oh' addressing someone; amanza is an archaic/poetic vocative meaning something like 'beloved' or 'love' (Dante's usage), del primo amante = 'of the first lover' (genitive phrase modifying amanza).
segments.paradiso-4-g124: o diva = a second vocative alternative 'O divine one' (diva = feminine form addressing a goddess-like or exalted figure). The short line marks a double address.
segments.paradiso-4-g125: diss’ io appresso = diss' is the contracted past tense of dire ('I said'); io is the subject 'I'; appresso here functions adverbially 'afterward/thereupon/next' (appresso advances the narrative: 'I then said').
segments.paradiso-4-g126: il cui parlar = 'whose speech' (cui as the relative pronoun in the sense 'whose'); m’inonda = mi + inonda, present tense 'floods/overwhelms me' (inonda used metaphorically); e scalda sì = 'and warms so' (sì = 'so' / 'to such a degree'). Note the possessive construction il cui + noun.
segments.paradiso-4-g127: che più e più m’avviva = che (introducing result) 'that' + più e più 'more and more' + m’avviva = mi + avviva (present subjunctive/indicative sense here as a simple present 'enlivens me' in stylistic register). The phrase expresses increasing enlivening.
segments.paradiso-4-g128: non è l’affezion mia tanto profonda = literal word order: non + è (present 'is not') + l'affezion mia ('my affection') + tanto profonda ('so deep'). Affezion is a medieval spelling of 'affezione/affection' meaning attachment/feeling.
segments.paradiso-4-g129: che basti a render voi grazia per grazia = che + basti (present subjunctive of bastare used in a potential/limiting sense 'that would suffice') + a render voi (a + infinitive render = 'to render/return to you') + grazia per grazia ('grace for grace' / 'favor for favor'). The subjunctive expresses that the speaker's affection might be insufficient.
segments.paradiso-4-g130: ma quei che vede e puote a ciò risponda = ma 'but' + quei che 'he who' (quei used as 'that one/who') + vede e puote ('sees and can/is able' — puote is an archaic present meaning 'is able') + a ciò risponda (a ciò = 'to that' / risponda is present subjunctive used optatively/imperatively: 'let him answer').
segments.paradiso-4-g131: Io veggio ben = Io 'I' + veggio (poetic variant of 'vedo', 'I see') + ben 'well' (emphatic). che già mai non si sazia nostro intelletto = subordinate clause: che 'that' + già mai non (negative with mai giving 'ever' in negative context) + si sazia (reflexive 'is satisfied') + nostro intelletto (subject 'our intellect'). The construction states a general truth in present tense.
segments.paradiso-4-g132: se ’l ver non lo illustra di fuor dal qual nessun vero si spazia = se 'if' + 'l ver (the truth) + non lo illustra (does not illuminate it; lo = 'it' = our intellect) + di fuor dal qual (literary phrasing 'from outside of which') + nessun vero si spazia (no truth ranges beyond/over it). Illustra here is used in the sense 'shed light upon/illuminate.'
segments.paradiso-4-g133: Posasi in esso = posasi is a past or perfect-form (literary) meaning 'it placed/settled itself' (reflexive sense implied) and in esso = 'in it' (i.e., the truth settles into the intellect). The verb is used impersonally/transitively in a poetic register.
segments.paradiso-4-g134: come fera in lustra = simile comparison: come = 'like/as,' fera = 'wild beast' (simile subject), in lustra = 'in its lair/pen' (lustra here is an older poetic noun; the phrase describes the beast settling into its accustomed place).
segments.paradiso-4-g135: tosto che giunto l’ha = tosto che = 'as soon as' (conjunction), giunto = past participle 'having arrived/reached,' l’ = clitic 'it' (the place/thing), ha = auxiliary 'has' (he/it has). The clause literally 'as soon as it has reached it.'
segments.paradiso-4-g136: e giugner puollo = e 'and' + giugner (infinitive or present infinitival sense 'to arrive/come') + puollo (archaic/uncertain word — contextually an emphatic or modal particle expressing that arrival would quickly occur). The line uses an old/poetic verb form and an uncommon particle.
segments.paradiso-4-g137: se non, = 'if not' (se = if; non = not). The comma indicates the conditional clause that follows will show the consequence if that were not so.
segments.paradiso-4-g138: ciascun disio sarebbe frustra = ciascun 'each/every' + disio 'desire' (subject) + sarebbe (conditional of essere 'would be') + frustra = 'in vain' (adverb). The conditional states the consequence: every desire would be vain.

sentences.paradiso-4-s17: That our justice appears unjust in the eyes of mortals is an argument of faith and not of heretical wickedness.
sentences.paradiso-4-s18: But because your perception can well penetrate this truth, as you desire, I will make you content.
sentences.paradiso-4-s19: If violence is when the one who suffers gives nothing to the one who forces, these souls were not excused by that; for the will, if it does not wish, does not extinguish itself, but behaves as nature behaves in fire, even if violence twists it a thousand times.
sentences.paradiso-4-s20: Therefore, if it (the will) bends much or little, force follows; and so these, being able, flee back into the holy place.
sentences.paradiso-4-s6: Still you have reason to doubt: that the souls seem to return to the stars, according to Plato's opinion.
sentences.paradiso-4-s7: These are the questions that arise equally in your will; and therefore first I will treat that one which has the most gall (bitterness).
sentences.paradiso-4-s8: Of the Seraphim: he who is most enraged, Moses, Samuel, and that John whom you wish to choose — I mean (not) Mary — do not have their seats in another heaven except these spirits which now appear to you; nor do they have, in their being, more or fewer years; but all make the first circle fair, and they have sweet life to differing degrees by feeling the eternal breath more or less.
sentences.paradiso-4-s25: Many times already, brother, it happened that, in order to flee danger, contrary to his liking he did that which it was not fitting to do; like Almeone, who, urged to this by his father, put his own mother to death — so as not to lose pity he made himself pitiless.
sentences.paradiso-4-s26: At this point I want you to think that force mixes itself with the will,
sentences.paradiso-4-s27: Absolute will does not consent to harm; but it consents to it only insofar as it fears, if it withdraws, to fall into greater anguish.
sentences.paradiso-4-s28: Therefore, when Piccarda presses that point, she understands the absolute will, and I (understand) the other; so that together we speak truly.
sentences.paradiso-4-s1: Between two foods, equally distant and enticing in the same way, one would die of hunger before any free man would bring one of them to his teeth; so a lamb would stand between two cravings of fierce wolves, fearing them both alike; so a dog would stand between two ladies: therefore, if I had been silent, I do not reproach myself, having been driven from my doubts in one way, since it was necessary, nor do I praise it.
sentences.paradiso-4-s2: I kept silent, but my desire, pictured on my face, and the pleading that accompanied it was written there — much warmer than any distinct spoken petition.
sentences.paradiso-4-s3: Beatrice did as Daniel did when Nebuchadnezzar, moved by anger, had unjustly made him a villain; and she said: 'I see well how one desire and another pulls you, so that your care binds itself, so that it cannot breathe forth.'
sentences.paradiso-4-s4: You object: 'If good will endures, why should another's violence diminish my merit or lessen my due measure?'
sentences.paradiso-4-s21: If their will had been whole, as Lorenzo held himself on the step, and as Muzio made his hand stern, thus he would have driven them back along the road from which they had been drawn, as they were loosened; but such a steadfast will is too rare.
sentences.paradiso-4-s22: And because of these words, if you have gathered them up thus, the argument is vain that would have caused you annoyance even more often.
sentences.paradiso-4-s23: But now another step crosses before your eyes, such that by yourself you would not get out; first you would be exhausted.
sentences.paradiso-4-s24: I have firmly set in my mind that a blessed soul could not lie; for it is always close to the first truth; and afterward you could hear from Piccarda that the affection of the veil kept Constancy; so that she seems here to contradict me.
sentences.paradiso-4-s13: He says that the soul returns to its own star, believing that that star is thereby determined when Nature gave it form; and perhaps his judgment is of another kind than the voice utters, and it may be (so) with an intention not to be derided.
sentences.paradiso-4-s14: If he intends to turn back to these wheels the honor of influence and the blame, perhaps in some true sweep of his bow he strikes.
sentences.paradiso-4-s15: This principle, badly understood, has almost already twisted the whole world, so that Jupiter, Mercury and Mars were passed over in naming.
sentences.paradiso-4-s16: The other doubt that moves you has less venom, for its malice could not lead you away from me elsewhere.
sentences.paradiso-4-s9: They appeared here, not because this sphere is their origin, but to give a sign of the heavenly sphere that has the lesser ascent.
sentences.paradiso-4-s10: One must speak thus to your intellect, because only from what is sensible does one learn what afterward is fitting for the intellect.
sentences.paradiso-4-s11: For this reason Scripture stoops to your capacity, and attributes feet and hand to God while meaning something else; and Holy Church, with a human appearance, presents Gabriel and Michael to you, and the other who restored Tobias to health.
sentences.paradiso-4-s12: What Timaeus argues about the souls is not like what one sees here, because, as he says, it seems that he perceives.
sentences.paradiso-4-s33: From that (cause) doubt arises, like a young shoot at the foot of truth; and it is nature that drives us from neck to neck up toward the summit.
sentences.paradiso-4-s34: This urges me, this assures me, with reverence, lady, to ask you about another truth which is obscure to me.
sentences.paradiso-4-s35: I want to know whether man can satisfy you for vows that are lacking with other goods, so that to your balance they may not seem equal.
sentences.paradiso-4-s36: Beatrice looked at me with eyes full of sparks of love so divine, that, overcome, my virtue gave way in my loins, and I almost lost myself with bowed eyes.
sentences.paradiso-4-s29: Such was the undulation of the holy stream that issued from the spring whence every truth originates; such did it place one desire and the other in peace.
sentences.paradiso-4-s30: "O beloved of the first lover, O divine one," I then said, "whose speech floods and warms me so, that it enlivens me more and more, my affection is not so deep as to repay you grace for grace; but let him who sees and can do so answer to that."
sentences.paradiso-4-s31: I see well that our intellect is never satisfied, if truth does not illuminate it from outside, from which no truth ranges beyond.
sentences.paradiso-4-s32: It (the truth) places itself in it, like a wild beast in its lair, as soon as it has reached it; and it would have arrived — if not, every desire would be in vain.
